chapters.title: It all began with a Star Wars movie
chapters.text:
Adam Savage has been collecting skills since he was a kid. The Star Wars universe opened the door to making for him, since the costumes and props were a kind of magic. Are there people whose job it is to create those? young Adam asked. From that day, he knew he had to become one of them.

After decades of building movie props, cosplay costumes, and his show Mythbusters, Adam picked up more skills than he ever imagined. His road to becoming a full-time maker was not smooth, but every wrong and right turn taught him something. Now he wants to share those lessons with anyone dreaming of turning making into a profession.

We are all makers, whether we admit it or not. We shape something out of nothing more often than we realize, adding to the world every day.

There is a maker inside you. Creating is at the heart of being human.

To prove the point, Adam tells the story of his life and the lessons he picked up along the way. He explores the making process across many fields, including how to find the motivation to start, how creators work and spot patterns, how to handle failure and mistakes, and how to organize tools and creative spaces.

Discover dozens of Adam's trades and passions, thousands of his projects, and the mistakes he made while building them. In this mix of stories and practical lessons, find what fits your own path as a maker.

chapters.title: Open your eyes to the maker in you
chapters.text:
Whenever you find yourself asking how to start your creative journey, re-read the question. Adam bets that what you really mean is, how do I start when I have no idea what to make? The subject is often the biggest hurdle. So step one is to find the pull that moves you.

At the start of Adam's journey, there was an obsession. A strong obsession helps a maker push past inaction, since passion gives you fuel. Adam had many over the years, from history and cosplay to LEGOs and science fiction. Each one opened a rabbit hole of exploration. He was lucky to grow up around parents and teachers who encouraged him to chase every small curiosity.

That is how Adam learned that many topics, objects, and processes can grab your attention. He calls them the secret thrills. They can lead your mind into places you never knew existed. Notice them and feed them, and ideas will start to appear. Whether it is storytelling or woodwork, they will reshape how you see and interpret the world. Adam figured out early on that making things with his hands was his superpower.

If you follow your passions down the rabbit hole into adulthood, they can bloom from hobbies into a real career.

Following his obsessions led Adam to one of his deepest passions, prop replication. Movies had always thrilled him, and this was a way to step into a film's world. On a visit to a Stanley Kubrick exhibit, one prop caught his eye, Major Kong's survival pack from Dr. Strangelove. What if I made one of those? He had to chase the idea from start to finish. That little spark from one afternoon led him to finish the project decades later.

Adam Savage has noted that whenever we reach out and create something from nothing, whether it is a chair or a poem, we are giving a piece of ourselves to the world.

chapters.title: Not every tool lives on a workbench
chapters.text:
Nobody said completing your dream projects would be easy. In fact, obstacles and limits pop up all the time, from time pressure to plain disorganization. Decades in the making business taught Adam that some tools and habits are essential to the creative process.

It all begins with a list. Some makers think lists kill creativity, but Adam argues the opposite. He remembers one project in particular, his obsession with building a replica of Stanley Kubrick's 2001: A Space Odyssey space suit. The build was incredibly complex and took him four years. It would have been impossible without lists as a planning tool. Lists captured the full scope of the work and helped him focus his energy on building, rather than trying to keep every detail in his head.

Rather than one giant list, Adam makes dozens and adds more as the project grows. He often spreads all his lists across the floor to see the full scope of what is still left to do.

Lists keep the project moving. They raise your chances of bringing things to the finish line, one step at a time.

Checkboxes are another vital tool in Adam's process. This simple step of marking off boxes as tasks are done has boosted his efficiency in a big way.

Color a checkbox when you finish a task. Color half a box when you only get partway through. Leave the box empty if the task is still waiting.

Adam first saw the real power of checkboxes while making a feature film. A process that can run for two years cannot survive without steady lists and checkboxes to track progress and capture every detail. Checkboxes let the makers on set know where the project stood without spending hours recalling every crossed-off task. And crossing things off is its own reward.

Then there is time. Deadlines can feel scary, but Adam believes in them firmly. Setting deadlines focuses your effort and strips away your excuses. Even when MythBusters was his full-time job, Adam kept his personal projects moving by setting deadlines that mattered to him, like finishing a costume in time for Comic-Con.

chapters.title: You are your own worst enemy
chapters.text:
Every creative person has their flaws. Adam's is impatience. He rushes through the process, takes every shortcut he can, and then ends up with plenty of literal and figurative stitches. The creative process is never a straight line. Plenty of things slow you down along the way. Skipping the time to set up your space, your tools, and your thinking can hurt the project. After decades of work, Adam learned to refine his process instead of charging through it.

As your craft turns into a profession, paid commissions will come. People will ask you to build things for money. Try not to panic or drown in imposter syndrome. Looking back on his first serious job, Adam sees his biggest mistake clearly. He never asked for help. He loved helping others, but asking for anything in return felt like failure. That kept him from learning, growing, and teaming up with other great makers.

When self-doubt takes over, summon the patience and humility to ask other makers for help.

Another lesson Adam learned the hard way, when the scope of his work grew, he had to learn to delegate. Being a poor delegator made him a worse boss. It kept him from passing skills to younger makers and helping them grow. Delegating is also about asking for help and finding the joy of working together. Be sure to appreciate and acknowledge the contributions of the people you work with. That gives them fuel to keep going.

Even then, Adam warns you that you will still mess up. Creation is a constant trial-and-error process, so do not despair if you do not nail it on the first try. Mistakes are part of being human. Adam learned to stop treating his attempts as failures and start treating them as experiments. You have to test many ideas before you find the ones that work best for you.

Creativity does not run in a straight, unbroken line. It twists and turns and doubles back, as Adam Savage has written.

Adam's advice is to build up your mistake tolerance as you grow. Give yourself the time and space to screw up and try again. If you are learning to build something, buy twice as many raw materials as you think you need. The process will never run as smoothly as you hope, so plan for it.

chapters.title: Never keep your secrets to yourself
chapters.text:
Sharing is part of how any society moves forward. The same rule applies to makers. Contributing back to the creative community makes you a link in a long chain, the makers who came before and the ones yet to be born. Adam has embraced this in every way he can, from open-source knowledge to open-door policies at his workshop.

It started during Adam's time at Tisch film school. He became part of a community that encouraged the free flow of information, passions, and opportunities. That stretch of life opened him up to a world of creative collaboration, where many people cared about the same things he did. His first workplaces were a different story. People were tired and overworked, and they rarely shared what they knew or showed interest in their work. That all changed when Adam met Jamie, his first great boss in the prop world. Jamie was his biggest champion. He shared his goals and interests and let Adam experiment in the workshop during his free time.

Once you find an interest, share it with your coworkers or fellow creators. You will learn more from their experience.

Another lesson Adam lives by is to share your work once you start making it. In the makers' world, word of mouth matters a lot and can pull in new projects. Do not be afraid of sounding confident about what you have built. There are many ways to put your work out there, like starting a blog, attending exhibitions, and representing yourself in line with your specialty.

Not every creator is eager to share, though. Some keep their process private, worrying it could hurt their job prospects or make them less special. Adam is the opposite. He is open about what he has learned because he wants to help others move forward.

It took him several years to finish a replica of Guillermo del Toro's Mecha Glove, a legendary prop, before he unveiled it at Comic-Con. Yet Adam did not feel right leaving the project there. He made a step-by-step guide for building the glove so others could share the joy of creation. That simple act of sharing gave Adam more happiness than he expected. As a maker, he sees it as his mission to give others the freedom to experiment and grow with the same knowledge.

chapters.title: Pick your tools and shape your space
chapters.text:
Adam believes your creative space is more than just an arrangement of tools. It reflects who you are as a maker. It shows how you think, how you approach creation, and what you value. It is more than a studio or a shop.

It is a tired stereotype that makers always live in messy, chaotic spaces that mirror their minds. Experienced creators know that clean space equals a clear mind, which lets you organize and create more efficiently. Adam's first workspaces were rough, stuffed with tools, ideas, and scrap. Each new skill brought new tools, paint, and glue, piling on more clutter. With time and exposure to other creators' spaces, he began to picture his own ideal setup.

He organized his workspace around two simple rules.

Keep clutter to a minimum, so you can see everything at a glance.

Make sure every tool is within easy reach.

These two principles help Adam stay in control of his space. That is why he is not a fan of drawers, since tools tend to die in there, never to be seen again. Another pet peeve of his is having tools scattered in different rooms. Every time that happens, he loses minutes hunting for them. The workspace itself has to support the making process, so adjust it and pay attention.

Adam also recommends tracking the tools and materials you reach for most. Cardboard has been his obsession from day one, since it is cheap, versatile, and full of possibilities. He loves using cardboard to build mock-ups of ideas, which he can show to clients. When he started renovating his house, he even built a cardboard mock-up of the home itself. Cardboard gives you a simple way to picture something before you build it for real.

Figure out which materials and tools work best for experimenting with new ideas.

Curating your tool collection also keeps your space clean. Adam believes strongly in cleaning your area after each workday, no matter how much you dislike the chore. Imagine skipping the morning mess cleanup and getting straight to work instead. Your space reflects who you are, so take care of it.

chapters.title: The long road to becoming a maker
chapters.text:
Adam Savage's success as a maker did not come easy. The path of creation is bumpy and complicated, but it never stopped him from building a real career as a craftsman. It all started in childhood, with each curiosity, from Star Wars to cardboard, sending him down a new road to explore.

Adam encourages you to follow your passions and chase what excites you. Research. Get curious about how things are built, and you will pick up the skills to create something of your own. As you figure out what drives you, refine the process. Adam loves lists, checkboxes, deadlines, and tidy spaces as essential tools for planning and finishing each project. With their help, he still found time and space for his side projects, all while running MythBusters as his full-time job.

That does not mean he never failed. He did, plenty of times. He chose to treat his mistakes as experiments, which helped him build a sharper process. Never be shy about asking for help or learning from others, and always pass your knowledge to the next wave of creators.

Give this a try.

Push deeper into the interests you already have. Get to know the full story behind them.

If you have an ongoing project, break it into several lists that cover the full scope of the work. Set deadlines that matter to you and tick the boxes as you finish each task.

Reflect on how you are a creator in your own life. What do you make from scratch? Does creating make you happy?

Look at your maker's space. Does it reflect your practice and your experience? What small changes would make it work better?

Keep learning from others, asking for help, and sharing what you know with anyone who is curious.

Read it till the end?
